title: Design Top-K Users and Videos Analytics for YouTube

transcript.text: Design Top-K Users and Videos Analytics for YouTube

blocks.md:
## 1. Requirements (Functional + Non-Functional)

> 🎙️ "Here's how I'll structure this: I'll lock the requirements and rough scale, sketch the API and data model, draw the high-level design one requirement at a time, then go deep on the two or three hardest parts — the sliding window and exact-vs-approximate top-K — checking in with you as I go. Sound good?"

The core tension: we're ingesting a firehose of view events (up to several hundred thousand per second) but must answer leaderboard queries in well under a second. That means we can't compute top-K at read time by scanning everything — we have to pre-aggregate on the write path and keep the read path tiny. I'll target an Amazon-style bar — so I'll make cost, operations, and "decide and move" first-class — tell me if you'd rather I tilt toward raw scale (Meta) or one deep distributed-systems problem (Google).

**Functional (the core flows — "above the line"):**

1. **Ingest a continuous stream of view events** (user_id, video_id, timestamp, view_duration_seconds).
   - 🗣️ Plain words: "Every time someone watches part of a video, the player sends us a little record saying who watched what and for how long — and we have to swallow hundreds of thousands of those a second without dropping any."
   - ⚡ Why it's hard: at several hundred thousand events/second, no single machine can accept the writes; we need a durable buffer that absorbs bursts so a slow downstream consumer never causes data loss.

2. **Maintain an ever-growing all-time total view-duration per user.**
   - 🗣️ Plain words: "For each user we keep a running grand total of all the watch-seconds they've ever generated, so we can rank the all-time biggest contributors."
   - ⚡ Why it's hard: this is an unbounded counter set across billions of users that must survive forever and survive crashes — an in-memory-only counter would lose data on restart, so it needs durable, idempotent increments.

3. **Maintain a sliding-window total view-duration per video for the most recent 15 minutes.**
   - 🗣️ Plain words: "For each video we keep a total of watch-seconds, but only counting the last 15 minutes — older watching 'falls off' the back of the window as time moves forward."
   - ⚡ Why it's hard: a true sliding window means values must continuously expire; naively recomputing 15 minutes of events on every query is far too slow, so we need a bucketed (time-sliced) aggregate that ages out cheaply.

4. **Serve a top-K query: given K (≤100), return top-K users (all-time) and top-K videos (last 15 min).**
   - 🗣️ Plain words: "A dashboard asks 'give me the top 50' and we hand back the ranked list almost instantly."
   - ⚡ Why it's hard: top-K over millions of keys can't scan everything per request; we maintain a bounded heap / sorted structure so the answer is essentially precomputed and the read is O(K).

**Below the line (out of scope):** per-video lifetime analytics dashboards, demographic breakdowns, watch-time graphs over arbitrary ranges, recommendation, billing/monetization, authentication of the dashboard beyond a token check, and historical replay of arbitrary past windows.

**Non-Functional:**

1. **Low-latency reads — p99 under ~200ms, target sub-second end to end.**
   - 🗣️ Plain words: "When the dashboard asks for a leaderboard, 99 times out of 100 it comes back faster than the blink-and-you-miss-it threshold."
   - 💥 What breaks without it: the creator dashboard feels broken and laggy, refreshes pile up, and the read tier melts under retries.

2. **High write throughput / horizontal scalability — sustain ~300K events/s, burst higher, scale by adding nodes.**
   - 🗣️ Plain words: "We can handle hundreds of thousands of view events a second, and if traffic doubles we just add more machines rather than rewrite anything."
   - 💥 What breaks without it: the ingestion buffer backs up, events queue for minutes, and the 15-minute leaderboard goes stale or wrong.

3. **Durability & fault tolerance — tolerate node failure with no loss of leaderboard correctness; aggregates survive crashes.**
   - 🗣️ Plain words: "If a server dies mid-stream, we don't lose anybody's watch-time and the rankings stay correct once it recovers."
   - 💥 What breaks without it: a single crashed aggregator silently drops a chunk of counts and a creator's all-time rank is permanently wrong — unforgivable for a metric people trust.

4. **Availability — ~99.9% for reads (about 8.7 hours/year down), the write/ingest path ~99.99%.**
   - 🗣️ Plain words: "The dashboard is essentially always up, and the event intake is even more reliable than the dashboard."
   - 💥 What breaks without it: dropped ingest windows create permanent gaps in the all-time totals — far worse than a dashboard being briefly unreadable.

5. **Cost-efficiency — favor approximate structures where exactness isn't required; bound memory.**
   - 🗣️ Plain words: "We don't pay to store every single event forever when a compact summary gives the same ranking."
   - 💥 What breaks without it: keeping raw events in RAM for billions/day blows the memory budget and the bill, with no ranking benefit.

⚠️ Gaps I'd flag out loud: a strict 15-minute sliding window is only as accurate as our bucket granularity — with 1-second buckets it's exact, with coarser buckets it's approximate at the edge. I'll call that trade-off out in the deep dive rather than pretend it's free.

> 🎙️ Script: "So we've got two leaderboards with very different shapes. One's an ever-growing all-time total per user — that's a durability problem. The other's a rolling 15-minute window per video — that's a data-expiry problem. Underneath both is a firehose of events we have to ingest without loss and answer queries on in under a second. The whole game is doing the aggregation on the write path so the read is basically free, and being honest about where we trade exactness for cost."

## 2. Clarifying Questions & Assumptions

1. **"When you say top-K all-time and top-K last-15-min — how exact do these need to be? Is an approximate ranking that's off by a fraction of a percent acceptable, or do creators see exact numbers they'll dispute?"**
   - 🗣️ Why I'm asking: "If approximate is fine I can use compact sketches and save a fortune in memory; if it has to be exact I need durable per-key counters, which is heavier."
   - ↔️ Design fork: If APPROXIMATE → Count-Min Sketch + heap, tiny memory, great for the 15-min window. If EXACT → durable per-key counters in a sharded store (Redis + Kafka-backed log), heavier but correct.
   - Assumption: all-time user totals must be **exact** (creators trust them); the 15-minute video window can be **near-exact** (1-second buckets, effectively exact) and I'll keep approximation as a cost lever.

2. **"What's the peak event rate and the average — and how bursty is it? Are we sizing for 300K/s steady or 300K/s peak with spikes?"**
   - 🗣️ Why I'm asking: "Burstiness decides how much buffer I need in the queue and whether I autoscale consumers."
   - ↔️ Design fork: If STEADY → fixed consumer fleet. If BURSTY → over-provision Kafka partitions and autoscale the stream-processing consumers on lag.
   - Assumption: up to several hundred thousand/s with real bursts → size Kafka for peak, autoscale consumers on consumer-lag.

3. **"Is the 15-minute window a hard tumbling boundary or a true sliding window that updates continuously?"**
   - 🗣️ Why I'm asking: "A true sliding window is more expensive — I need fine-grained buckets that expire second by second, not a single counter that resets every 15 minutes."
   - ↔️ Design fork: If TUMBLING → cheap, one counter per 15-min epoch. If SLIDING → ~900 one-second buckets per video (a ring buffer) summed on read.
   - Assumption: **true sliding** window (the problem says "most recent 15-minute window") → bucketed ring buffer.

4. **"Roughly how many distinct videos are 'active' in any 15-minute window, and how many total users do we rank all-time?"**
   - 🗣️ Why I'm asking: "These set sizes decide whether the top-K structures fit in memory or need sharding."
   - ↔️ Design fork: If MILLIONS of active videos → shard the window store + merge top-K. If THOUSANDS → single node is fine.
   - Assumption: ~tens of millions of active videos in a window, ~2B users all-time.

5. **"Can a single event be delivered more than once, and do we need exactly-once counting, or is at-least-once with dedup acceptable?"**
   - 🗣️ Why I'm asking: "If the producer can retry, double-counting will corrupt totals unless I make increments idempotent."
   - ↔️ Design fork: If DUPLICATES POSSIBLE → carry an event_id and dedup with a TTL store. If GUARANTEED ONCE → skip dedup.
   - Assumption: **at-least-once** delivery → every event carries an `event_id`; consumers dedup.

6. **"How fresh must the 15-minute leaderboard be — is a few seconds of lag behind real time acceptable?"**
   - 🗣️ Why I'm asking: "A small staleness budget lets me batch and cache; zero lag forces synchronous updates."
   - ↔️ Design fork: If SECONDS OK → micro-batch + 1–2s cache TTL. If REAL-TIME → update structures per event, no read cache.
   - Assumption: **1–2 seconds** of lag is fine for both leaderboards.

7. **"Do we need multi-region, or is a single region with cross-AZ redundancy enough?"**
   - 🗣️ Why I'm asking: "Multi-region changes the write topology and how I keep counters consistent."
   - ↔️ Design fork: If MULTI-REGION → regional aggregation + a global merge step. If SINGLE → multi-AZ replicas only.
   - Assumption: **single region, multi-AZ** to start; I'll note the multi-region extension.

8. **"Is K truly capped at 100, and are the K values arbitrary per request?"**
   - 🗣️ Why I'm asking: "A small bounded K means I can keep a tiny top-N heap instead of a full sort."
   - ↔️ Design fork: If K≤100 → maintain a top-~100 (say top-200 for safety) structure and slice. If K unbounded → must keep a full sorted index.
   - Assumption: **K ≤ 100** → maintain top-200 per shard and merge.

> 🤝 Checkpoint: "Those are my assumptions — exact all-time totals, near-exact sliding window, at-least-once with dedup, single region to start. Anything there you'd push back on before I size it?"

## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers)

I'll compute only the few numbers that drive a decision. (QPS — queries per second, how many requests hit a server each second. TTL — time-to-live, how long a cached value lives before it expires.)

| Metric | Derivation (step-by-step) | Rounded | Talkable phrase | Decision it drives |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Peak ingest rate | given several hundred thousand/s | 300K/s | "about three hundred thousand events a second" | Single node can't accept writes → durable queue + sharded consumers |
| Events/day | 300K/s × 86,400 s/day | 25.9B | "roughly 26 billion events a day" | Can't store raw events in RAM → aggregate, don't retain raw |
| Raw daily ingest bytes | ~50 bytes/event × 25.9B | ~1.3 TB/day | "about a terabyte and a third a day raw" | Raw log is cheap on disk (Kafka), expensive in RAM → aggregate in memory only |
| Ingest shard count | 300K/s ÷ ~150K ops/s per Redis node | 2 (→ over-provision to 4–8) | "two nodes at the limit, so I'll run several" | A single Redis tops out ~150K ops/s → must shard the counters |
| All-time user counters | 2B users × ~16 bytes (id+counter) | ~32 GB | "about thirty gigs of counters" | Fits across a small sharded Redis cluster in RAM |
| Sliding-window memory | ~10M active videos × 900 one-sec buckets × ~8 bytes | ~72 GB | "tens of gigs for the window" | Bounded and shardable → ring-buffer per video is viable |
| Dashboard query rate | thousands of concurrent → assume | ~5K QPS | "a few thousand reads a second" | Reads are tiny vs writes → cache top-K, read path is O(K) |

The ONE number that forces the design: **300K events/s vs ~150K ops/s per Redis node.** That's the flip — above ~150K/s you cannot use a single counter node, so consistent-hash sharding of counters and a partitioned Kafka topic become mandatory. Storage on disk is a non-issue (a terabyte a day of raw log is cheap and we trim it); the real constraint is write throughput and bounding in-memory aggregate size.

> 🎙️ Script: "The number that matters is about three hundred thousand events a second coming in, against maybe a few thousand leaderboard reads a second going out. So this is a write-heavy problem — the reads are almost free. One Redis node caps out around a hundred fifty thousand ops a second, so I'm going to shard the counters and the event stream from the start. I'll spend my whole budget on the write and aggregation path."

## 4. Core Entities

This is a first draft of the entities; I'll add full field definitions in §7.

- **ViewEvent** — the raw fact that someone watched part of a video: who, what, when, how long. It owns nothing long-term; it's the input that drives every aggregate.
  - 🗣️ Simple analogy: "Think of it like a single receipt that drops into a shoebox every time someone watches — we tally the receipts, we don't keep them forever."
  - ⚠️ Interviewer probe: "What if the same event arrives twice?" → it carries an `event_id`; the consumer dedups so we never double-count.

- **UserViewTotal** — the durable, ever-growing sum of view-seconds for one user, all time. Owns the all-time leaderboard input.
  - 🗣️ Simple analogy: "Like a lifetime odometer on a car — it only ticks up, never resets."
  - ⚠️ Interviewer probe: "How does this survive a crash?" → increments come from a durable log (Kafka) and land in a replicated store, so a restart replays rather than loses.

- **VideoWindowAggregate** — the rolling 15-minute sum of view-seconds for one video, stored as ~900 one-second buckets (a ring buffer). Owns the last-15-min leaderboard input.
  - 🗣️ Simple analogy: "Like a 15-minute hourglass that's actually 900 tiny one-second compartments — each second the oldest grain falls out the back."
  - ⚠️ Interviewer probe: "How do you expire old data without a sweep job?" → each bucket has a timestamp; on read or write we ignore/zero buckets older than 15 minutes — expiry is implicit, no cron needed.

- **Leaderboard** — the materialized top-K result (top-200 users, top-200 videos) kept ready to serve, refreshed continuously.
  - 🗣️ Simple analogy: "Like a scoreboard on the wall that someone keeps updating, so when you ask 'who's winning?' you just read it."
  - ⚠️ Interviewer probe: "Why precompute instead of sorting on demand?" → sorting millions of keys per request can't hit sub-second; a maintained top-N heap makes the read O(K).

> 🎙️ Script: "There are really four things. The ViewEvent is the raw receipt flowing in. The UserViewTotal is a lifetime odometer per user — only goes up. The VideoWindowAggregate is the interesting one — it's a 15-minute hourglass built from 900 one-second buckets so old watch-time falls off the back automatically. And the Leaderboard is just the precomputed scoreboard we keep ready so reads are instant. I'll flesh out the exact fields and stores in the data model."

## 5. API / Interface

Default REST over HTTPS. The dashboard read endpoints identify the caller from a JWT (JSON Web Token — a signed token proving who the caller is); the ingest endpoint authenticates the player client and never trusts client-supplied totals.

| Method + Path | Auth | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| POST /v1/events | Client token | Ingest a view event |
| GET /v1/leaderboard/users?k=K | JWT | Top-K users all-time |
| GET /v1/leaderboard/videos?k=K&window=15m | JWT | Top-K videos last 15 min |

**POST /v1/events** — POST because it creates new state (records a new fact).
- Key request fields: `event_id: string` (client-generated UUID, so retries dedup — without it at-least-once delivery double-counts); `user_id: string` and `video_id: string` (the two keys we aggregate by); `duration_s: int` (the watch-seconds to add). The server does NOT trust a client-supplied total or rank — it only accepts the raw duration and aggregates server-side.
- Concrete example:
```
// Request
POST /v1/events
{ "event_id": "evt_9f3a", "user_id": "u_123", "video_id": "v_777", "duration_s": 42, "ts": "2026-06-20T14:32:01Z" }

// Response 202
{ "status": "ACCEPTED" }
```
- Key design decision: returns **202 Accepted**, not 200 — ingestion is asynchronous (we append to Kafka and return); the `event_id` makes the write idempotent so a retried POST is counted once.

**GET /v1/leaderboard/videos?k=50&window=15m** — GET because it only reads.
- Key request fields: `k: int` (how many to return, capped at 100 server-side — a client asking for 10,000 is clamped); `window` (fixed at 15m here). No user-supplied ranking is accepted.
- Concrete example:
```
// Request
GET /v1/leaderboard/videos?k=3&window=15m

// Response 200
{ "window": "15m", "as_of": "2026-06-20T14:32:05Z",
  "results": [
    { "rank": 1, "video_id": "v_777", "view_seconds": 184204 },
    { "rank": 2, "video_id": "v_512", "view_seconds": 173998 },
    { "rank": 3, "video_id": "v_900", "view_seconds": 159302 } ] }
```
- Key design decision: the response carries `as_of` so the dashboard knows the freshness; served from a cached materialized top-K with a 1–2s TTL so thousands of reads never touch the aggregation tier.

**GET /v1/leaderboard/users?k=50** — GET, reads the all-time board; same shape with `view_seconds` as the lifetime total.

> 🎙️ Script: "Three endpoints. Ingest is a POST that returns 202 because we just drop the event on the queue and aggregate later — and it carries a client-generated event_id so retries don't double-count. The two reads are plain GETs that hand back a cached top-K with an as_of timestamp, and I clamp K to 100 server-side so nobody can ask for a million rows. Crucially the client never sends a total or a rank — it sends raw watch-seconds and the server owns all the math, so nobody can game the board by editing a request."

## 6. High-Level Design (built one functional requirement at a time)

### 6.1 "Ingest a continuous stream of view events"

The player POSTs an event; we must accept it durably at 300K/s and hand it to aggregators without loss.

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Ingest API (stateless) | A thin fleet of web servers behind a load balancer that validate an event and append it to the log | Stateless servers scale horizontally trivially and do no aggregation, so they stay fast | Not writing straight to a DB (300K/s of row writes melts any DB); not aggregating in the API (couples write rate to compute) | One extra hop before durability |
| Kafka | A durable, partitioned append-only log that buffers a firehose so nothing is lost if a consumer is slow | We need a shock absorber that survives consumer crashes and replays — Kafka retains events and lets us add consumers | Not a plain in-memory queue (loses data on crash); not direct-to-Redis (no replay, no buffer for bursts) | A few seconds of pipeline latency |

**🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **Kafka** — a durable log that stores a stream of events in order and keeps them on disk for a retention period. Here it buffers the 300K/s firehose so a slow or crashed aggregator never loses events — we just replay.
> **Partition** — a shard of a Kafka topic; events with the same key land in the same partition. Here we partition by video_id (and a separate topic keyed by user_id) so all of one video's events are ordered and handled by one consumer.
> **Load balancer** — a router spreading incoming requests across the API fleet. Here it lets us add API nodes to absorb write spikes.

**Ingest flow**
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Client[Player client]
    Client -->|"1: POST /v1/events"| API[Ingest API]
    API -->|"2: append event"| Kafka[[Kafka topic]]
    Kafka -->|"3: consume"| Worker[Stream consumers]
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef queue fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    class Client client
    class API,Worker svc
    class Kafka queue
```

**Numbered step narration**
1. Player POSTs the event with an `event_id`. The Ingest API validates shape and auth — it owns admission, nothing else.
2. The API appends the event to Kafka, partitioned by video_id, and returns 202. State change: the event is now durably persisted and replayable. If Kafka is briefly unavailable the API buffers locally and applies backpressure rather than dropping.
3. Stream consumers pull batches. If a consumer crashes mid-batch, another takes over from the last committed offset — at-least-once, deduped downstream.

> 💬 "The key move here is we never aggregate in the request path — we just durably record the event and return, so write rate is decoupled from compute."

> 🎙️ Script: "Ingest is deliberately dumb and fast. The player hits a stateless API that does nothing but validate and append to Kafka — a durable log that buffers our whole firehose so a slow consumer can't lose anything. We return a 202 immediately. Kafka's the shock absorber; it lets us crash and replay, and it lets us add consumers when traffic spikes."

### 6.2 "Maintain an ever-growing all-time total per user"

Each event must add its duration to that user's lifetime total, durably and idempotently.

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| User-counter consumers | Stream workers reading the user-keyed topic and applying increments | They process partitions in parallel so throughput scales with partitions | Not a single aggregator (can't do 300K/s) | Per-partition ordering only |
| Redis (sharded) | An in-memory key-value store with atomic increment, sharded by user_id | We need millions of atomic INCRBY/s with sub-ms latency; Redis does exactly that | Not Postgres (300K row updates/s impossible); not DynamoDB (atomic increment exists but pricier at this rate) | In-memory → needs durability backing (AOF + Kafka replay) |
| Dedup store | A TTL set of recently-seen event_ids | Makes increments idempotent so at-least-once delivery doesn't double-count | Not trusting the broker for exactly-once (fragile across failures) | Small memory + a lookup per event |

**🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **Redis** — an in-memory key-value store that's extremely fast and supports atomic operations like INCRBY. Here it holds each user's lifetime counter and increments it in well under a millisecond.
> **Atomic increment (INCRBY)** — an add that can't be interrupted or lost between concurrent writers. Here it lets many consumers safely add to the same user's total.
> **AOF (Append-Only File)** — Redis's on-disk log of every write. Here it lets a crashed Redis node rebuild its counters instead of starting from zero.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Kafka[[Kafka user topic]]
    Kafka -->|"1: consume batch"| UC[User-counter consumers]
    UC -->|"2: check event_id"| Dedup[(Dedup TTL store)]
    UC -->|"3: INCRBY user total"| Redis[(Redis user counters)]
    Redis -->|"4: feed top-K"| LB[(User top-K heap)]
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef queue fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    class UC svc
    class Kafka queue
    class Dedup,Redis,LB db
```
↳ reuses existing: Kafka (from §6.1)

**Numbered step narration**
1. Consumers pull a batch from the user-keyed topic. They own the all-time aggregation.
2. For each event they check the dedup store; if `event_id` was seen, skip. State change: duplicate suppressed.
3. Otherwise INCRBY the user's counter in Redis. State change: that user's lifetime total rises by `duration_s`, durably (AOF + replayable from Kafka).
4. The consumer offers the new total to a maintained top-K structure (see §8). If Redis is down, consumers pause and Kafka holds events — no loss.

> 💬 "Lifetime totals are exact and durable — every increment is idempotent via the event_id and recoverable from the Kafka log."

> 🎙️ Script: "For the all-time board, consumers read events, dedup on the event_id so a retried event doesn't double-count, and do an atomic increment on that user's counter in Redis. Redis gives us millions of sub-millisecond increments a second. It's in memory, so I back it with its append-only file and the Kafka log — if a node dies, we replay rather than lose anyone's total."

### 6.3 "Maintain a sliding 15-minute total per video"

Same firehose, different math: per video, sum only the last 15 minutes, expiring old data continuously.

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Window consumers | Stream workers on the video-keyed topic | Parallel per-partition processing | Not single node | Per-partition ordering |
| Bucketed ring buffer in Redis | Per video, ~900 one-second buckets (a Redis hash) summed over the window | Lets old seconds expire implicitly — no sweep job; sum is cheap | Not one counter reset every 15m (that's tumbling, not sliding); not storing raw events (too much memory) | ~900 small fields per active video |

**🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **Sliding window** — a total over "the last N minutes" that continuously moves forward, unlike a fixed window that resets. Here it's the last 15 minutes of watch-seconds per video.
> **Ring buffer** — a fixed-size circular array where new data overwrites the oldest slot. Here each video has 900 one-second slots; second 901 overwrites slot 1, so memory is bounded.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Kafka[[Kafka video topic]]
    Kafka -->|"1: consume"| WC[Window consumers]
    WC -->|"2: add to current-second bucket"| Ring[(Redis ring buffer per video)]
    Ring -->|"3: sum last 900 buckets"| VLB[(Video top-K heap)]
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef queue fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    class WC svc
    class Kafka queue
    class Ring,VLB db
```
↳ reuses existing: Kafka (§6.1), Redis cluster (§6.2)

**Numbered step narration**
1. Window consumers pull events from the video topic. They own the rolling aggregate.
2. They add `duration_s` to the bucket for the event's current second (bucket index = epoch_second mod 900). State change: that one-second slot grows; the slot from 15 minutes ago is overwritten, so expiry is automatic.
3. Periodically (or on demand) the rolling total = sum of all 900 buckets, fed to the video top-K. If an event arrives late by more than 15 minutes, it's dropped (out of window) — acceptable per our staleness budget.

> 💬 "The ring buffer is the trick — old watch-time falls off the back automatically because we overwrite the slot from 15 minutes ago; no cleanup job ever runs."

> 🎙️ Script: "The sliding window is the fun part. Instead of one counter, each video gets 900 one-second buckets in a ring. We add each event to the current second's bucket, and the bucket from 15 minutes ago just gets overwritten — so expiry is free, no cron job. The 15-minute total is the sum of the 900 buckets. With one-second granularity it's essentially exact, and memory's bounded at 900 little numbers per active video."

### 6.4 "Serve top-K users and top-K videos"

The dashboard asks for top-K; we serve a precomputed, cached result in O(K).

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Top-K maintainer | Keeps a bounded top-200 sorted set per board, updated as counters change | Read becomes O(K) — no scanning millions of keys | Not sorting on read (can't hit sub-second over millions) | Slight lag between counter and board |
| Read API + cache | Serves the cached top-K with a 1–2s TTL | Thousands of identical reads collapse to one cache entry | Not hitting the aggregation tier per read (wasteful) | Up to ~2s staleness |

**🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **Sorted set (Redis ZSET)** — a structure that keeps members ordered by score with fast top-N reads. Here it holds the top-K leaderboard so a query is a single ZREVRANGE.
> **Cache TTL** — how long a cached answer is reused before refresh. Here 1–2s, so a burst of dashboard reads hits cache, not the heaps.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Dash[Dashboard client]
    Dash -->|"1: GET /leaderboard"| RAPI[Read API]
    RAPI -->|"2: cache hit?"| Cache[(Redis cache top-K)]
    Cache -.->|"3: miss, read board"| ZSET[(Top-K sorted set)]
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    class Dash client
    class RAPI svc
    class Cache,ZSET db
```
↳ reuses existing: Redis cluster (§6.2, §6.3)

**Numbered step narration**
1. Dashboard GETs the leaderboard with K. Read API owns clamping K≤100 and auth.
2. On cache hit (1–2s TTL) it returns immediately — the common case at thousands of QPS.
3. On miss it reads the top-K sorted set (ZREVRANGE 0 K), caches it, returns. If the board is briefly stale during a node failover, the `as_of` field tells the client.

> 💬 "Reads are basically free — a cached slice of a precomputed top-200; we never sort at query time."

> 🎙️ Script: "Serving is the easy half. We keep a precomputed top-200 sorted set for each board, so a query is just 'give me the top K from this sorted set' — O(K). And we cache that result for a second or two, so thousands of dashboard reads collapse onto one cache entry and the aggregation tier never feels them."

**Final (high-level)**
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Client[Player client]
    Dash[Dashboard client]
    Client -->|"1: POST event"| API[Ingest API]
    API -->|"2: append"| Kafka[[Kafka topics]]
    subgraph Write["🔄 Aggregation path"]
        Kafka -->|"3: consume"| UC[User consumers]
        Kafka -->|"3: consume"| WC[Window consumers]
        UC -->|"4: dedup"| Dedup[(Dedup TTL)]
        UC -->|"5: INCRBY"| RU[(Redis user counters)]
        WC -->|"6: bucket add"| RW[(Redis ring buffers)]
        RU -->|"7: update"| ZU[(User top-K ZSET)]
        RW -->|"8: update"| ZV[(Video top-K ZSET)]
    end
    subgraph Read["⚡ Read path"]
        Dash -->|"9: GET leaderboard"| RAPI[Read API]
        RAPI -->|"10: cache"| Cache[(Top-K cache)]
        Cache -.->|"11: miss"| ZU
        Cache -.->|"11: miss"| ZV
    end
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef queue fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    class Client,Dash client
    class API,UC,WC,RAPI svc
    class Kafka queue
    class Dedup,RU,RW,ZU,ZV,Cache db
```

> 🎙️ Script: "Write path: events land via a dumb API into Kafka, then two sets of consumers — one builds exact lifetime user totals with idempotent increments, the other builds the 900-bucket sliding window per video. Both feed precomputed top-200 sorted sets. Read path: the dashboard hits a cached slice of those sorted sets, so reads are O(K) and never touch the heavy aggregation. Kafka is the seam that decouples the firehose from the compute."

> 🤝 Checkpoint: "That's the skeleton end to end. The two hardest parts are the sliding-window expiry and keeping the top-K correct across shards — want me to go deep on the window first, or on exact-vs-approximate top-K at scale?"

## 7. Data Model & Storage

**PART A — ENTITY TABLE**

| Entity | Key fields | Chosen store | Shard/partition key | Consistency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ViewEvent | event_id (PK), user_id, video_id, ts, duration_s | Kafka log (transient) | video_id / user_id | Ordered per partition |
| UserViewTotal | user_id (PK), total_seconds | Redis (sharded) + AOF | user_id | Strong per key (atomic INCRBY) |
| VideoWindowAggregate | video_id (PK), buckets[900] | Redis (sharded) + AOF | video_id | Near-real-time |
| Dedup record | event_id (PK), ttl | Redis TTL set | event_id | Best-effort, TTL'd |
| UserTopK / VideoTopK | member, score | Redis ZSET | board name | Eventually consistent w/ counters |

**PART B — STORAGE DECISION CARDS**

**🗄️ Kafka — used for: ViewEvent ingestion**
1. What it is: a durable, ordered, append-only log that stores a stream of events on disk for a retention period and lets many consumers read independently.
2. Why we picked it: we need to absorb a 300K/s firehose without loss even when a consumer crashes, and we need replay to rebuild aggregates — that's exactly Kafka's job (durable buffer + offset replay + partition parallelism).
3. Considered instead: **RabbitMQ** — great for task queues but weaker at high-throughput replayable streams; **direct-to-Redis** — no durability buffer, a Redis hiccup loses the firehose; **Kinesis** — equivalent, but I'll keep it cloud-neutral.
4. Trade-off: a few seconds of pipeline latency and the operational cost of running a Kafka cluster.
5. 🗣️ "I'm putting Kafka right after ingest because it's a durable shock absorber — it swallows three hundred thousand events a second, survives a consumer dying, and lets me replay to rebuild a counter. I looked at pushing straight into Redis but then a Redis blip loses the stream, which I can't accept."

**🗄️ Redis (sharded) — used for: user counters, video ring buffers, top-K, dedup, cache**
1. What it is: an in-memory key-value store with atomic operations (INCRBY, sorted sets) and sub-millisecond latency, with on-disk persistence via AOF.
2. Why we picked it: we need millions of atomic increments per second and O(K) top-N reads — only an in-memory store with native atomic counters and sorted sets hits that latency at that rate.
3. Considered instead: **Postgres** — can't do 300K row updates/s; **DynamoDB** — has atomic counters but cost and per-op latency are worse at this volume; **Cassandra** — great write throughput but counters are eventually consistent and read-modify-write top-K is awkward.
4. Trade-off: it's in memory, so durability needs AOF + Kafka replay, and we must shard to get past ~150K ops/s per node.
5. 🗣️ "Redis does the heavy lifting — atomic increments for the lifetime totals, ring buffers for the window, sorted sets for the leaderboards. It's in memory so I back it with the append-only file and the Kafka log; if a node dies I replay rather than lose counts. And I shard by key because one node tops out around a hundred fifty thousand ops a second and I'm doing twice that."

**🗄️ Cold object storage (S3) — used for: long-term raw event archive (optional)**
1. What it is: cheap, durable blob storage for keeping raw events beyond Kafka retention.
2. Why we picked it: lets us re-derive any aggregate later or audit disputes without paying RAM.
3. Considered instead: keeping everything in Kafka forever (expensive disk), or discarding raw (no audit trail).
4. Trade-off: re-deriving from S3 is slow (batch), not real-time.
5. 🗣️ "I'll tier old events out of Kafka into cheap object storage so I can rebuild or audit any board later without keeping a terabyte a day in memory."

**PART C — Per-operation consistency**

| Operation | Store | Consistency | Why right here |
|---|---|---|---|
| Increment user total | Redis | Strong per key (atomic) | Lifetime totals must be exact |
| Add to window bucket | Redis | Near-real-time | 1–2s lag acceptable |
| Read leaderboard | Cache→ZSET | Eventual (≤2s) | Display can lag slightly |
| Dedup check | Redis TTL | Best-effort | Suppresses the common retry |
| Read-your-own-write (creator just watched) | Redis | Bounded ≤2s | We surface `as_of`; not a correctness path |

> 🎙️ Script: "Storage is two real stores plus a cold archive. Kafka is the durable intake log. Redis is everything hot — atomic counters for exact lifetime totals, ring buffers for the sliding window, and sorted sets for the boards — sharded by key and backed by its append-only file plus Kafka replay. I considered Postgres and Dynamo for the counters but neither hits hundreds of thousands of atomic increments a second cheaply. I tier raw events to object storage so I can rebuild or audit later. Everything that's money-like — the lifetime totals — is exact; everything display-like is eventually consistent within a second or two."

## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great

🆘 If you get stuck: go back to "what forces a single node to break" and reason forward — the firehose rate vs one node's ceiling is the spine of every answer here.

### How do we maintain a true 15-minute sliding window cheaply?

**#### Bad: Recompute from raw events on read**
🗣️ What this is: when someone asks for the leaderboard, scan the last 15 minutes of raw events and sum per video.
Approach: keep raw events, on query filter ts > now-15m, group by video, sum, sort.
Why people try this: it's obviously correct and simple.
⚠️ What breaks: at 300K events/s, 15 minutes is ~270M events to scan per query, at thousands of QPS — completely impossible for sub-second.
🔁 Forces upgrade: we must pre-aggregate, not scan.

**#### Good: One counter per video, reset every 15 minutes (tumbling)**
↩️ What the previous tier got wrong: scanning raw events was unbounded work; this pre-aggregates into a single counter so reads are O(1) per video.
🗣️ What this is: each video has one running counter that we wipe clean every 15 minutes.
Approach: INCRBY per event into one counter per video; a job resets all counters at each 15-minute boundary.
Why people try this: trivial and cheap.
⚠️ What breaks: it's a **tumbling** window, not sliding — right after a reset the board shows near-zero, and 14 minutes of recent watch-time vanishes instantly. The leaderboard flickers and is wrong for most of each window.
🔁 Forces upgrade: we need continuous expiry, not a cliff.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    E[Event] -->|INCRBY| C[(One counter per video)]
    Reset[15m timer] -.->|wipe| C
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
    class E svc
    class C db
```

**#### Great: Bucketed ring buffer (900 one-second buckets)**
↩️ What the previous tier got wrong: the tumbling counter expired everything at once; the ring buffer expires one second at a time so the window truly slides.
🗣️ What this is: each video keeps 900 little one-second tallies in a circle; each new second overwrites the one from 15 minutes ago, and the total is their sum.
Approach: bucket index = epoch_second mod 900. On event: add duration to bucket[idx], and if that bucket's stored second is stale, zero it first. Window total = sum of 900 buckets (or a maintained running sum updated by +new -evicted).
🔢 Decision-forcing math: 10M active videos × 900 buckets × 8 bytes ≈ 72 GB — bounded and shardable, versus ~270M raw events/query for the Bad approach.
✅ Failure matrix:

| Scenario | What happens |
|---|---|
| Event arrives on time | Added to current-second bucket; total updated |
| Event late <15m | Added to its correct older bucket if still in window |
| Event late >15m | Dropped — outside window (acceptable per staleness budget) |
| Redis node dies | Replay from Kafka rebuilds buckets; AOF shortens replay |
| Clock skew across consumers | Use event ts, not wall clock; NTP-synced; small skew tolerated |

> 🎙️ Script: "The window is a ring of 900 one-second buckets per video. Each event lands in the current second's slot, and the slot from 15 minutes back just gets overwritten — so old watch-time falls off the back automatically, no cleanup job. The total is the sum of the buckets, and I keep a running sum so it's O(1) to read. With one-second granularity it's essentially exact, and the memory is bounded — about 70 gigs across the cluster."
> 🧠 If they ask "why not a tumbling window — it's simpler?": "Tumbling resets to zero on a boundary, so for most of each window the board is missing up to 14 minutes of data and flickers. The ring buffer slides continuously, which is what 'last 15 minutes' actually means."

### How do we keep top-K correct and fast across shards?

**#### Bad: Sort all keys on every query**
🗣️ What this is: read every counter, sort, take K.
⚠️ What breaks: sorting millions/tens-of-millions of keys per request is way over the sub-second budget at thousands of QPS.
🔁 Forces upgrade: maintain the top-K incrementally.

**#### Good: One global sorted set updated per increment**
↩️ What the previous tier got wrong: sorting per read was O(N log N) per query; a sorted set keeps order incrementally so reads are O(K).
🗣️ What this is: a single Redis sorted set per board that we update whenever a counter changes; reads take the top K.
Approach: on each counter update, ZADD the new score; query is ZREVRANGE 0 K.
⚠️ What breaks: a single global ZSET is a hot key — every one of 300K updates/s hits one node, blowing past ~150K ops/s, and it's a single point of serialization.
🔁 Forces upgrade: we must shard the counters and merge.

**#### Great: Per-shard local top-K + merge (heap merge)**
↩️ What the previous tier got wrong: one global sorted set was a single hot node; sharding spreads updates and we merge small local top-Ks at read.
🗣️ What this is: each shard keeps its own top-200; to answer top-K we grab each shard's top-200 and merge them with a heap, which is tiny.
Approach: consistent-hash keys across S shards (consistent hashing — a scheme that maps keys to nodes so adding/removing a node only moves a small fraction of keys). Each shard maintains a local top-200 ZSET. A query fans out to S shards, each returns ≤200, and a merge step (a K-way heap merge) produces the global top-K. Because the true global top-K is always within the union of per-shard top-Ks (a key in the global top-K must be in its own shard's top-K), the merge is exact for K≤200.
🔢 Decision-forcing math: merge cost = S shards × 200 entries through a heap ≈ a few thousand comparisons — sub-millisecond — versus sorting tens of millions of keys.
✅ Failure matrix:

| Scenario | What happens |
|---|---|
| One shard down | Its slice missing; serve partial with `as_of` + degraded flag; replay on recovery |
| Hot video (one shard hammered) | That key's updates batched/coalesced; shard still ≤150K ops/s |
| K asked > 200 | Clamp to 100 (≤200 maintained), so merge stays exact |
| Tie scores | Break by id deterministically so paging is stable |

For approximate mode (cost lever), replace exact counters with a **Count-Min Sketch** (a compact array of counters hashed multiple ways that estimates a key's count with a small, bounded over-count) feeding a heap — orders of magnitude less memory when exactness isn't required.

> 🎙️ Script: "Top-K is per-shard-local-plus-merge. I consistent-hash keys across shards; each shard keeps its own top-200 in a sorted set. To answer a query I ask every shard for its top-200 and heap-merge them — that's exact for K up to 200 because a globally-top key has to be top on its own shard too. The merge is a few thousand comparisons, sub-millisecond. If exactness ever isn't worth the memory, I swap the counters for a Count-Min Sketch feeding a heap."
> 🧠 If they ask "is the merge actually exact?": "Yes for K ≤ the per-shard top size. A key in the global top-K can't be missing from its own shard's top-K, so the union always contains the true answer; I keep top-200 to cover K≤100 with margin."

### How do we ingest 300K/s without loss or hot partitions?

**#### Good: Single Kafka topic, partition by video_id**
🗣️ What this is: one topic split into partitions keyed by video so each video is ordered.
⚠️ What breaks: a viral video sends all its events to one partition → one consumer hot-spots.
🔁 Forces upgrade: spread hot keys.

**#### Great: Partition with salted hot keys + autoscaled consumers**
↩️ What the previous tier got wrong: keying purely by video sent a viral video to one partition; salting spreads it.
🗣️ What this is: for known-hot videos, split their events across a few sub-partitions and recombine in the aggregate.
Approach: partition key = video_id, but for hot keys append a salt (video_id#0..n) so load spreads across partitions; the aggregator sums the sub-keys. Consumers autoscale on consumer-lag (how far behind the latest offset they are).
🔢 Decision-forcing math: 300K/s ÷ ~50K/s per consumer ≈ 6 consumers steady; lag-based autoscaling adds more on bursts.
✅ Failure matrix:

| Scenario | What happens |
|---|---|
| Consumer crash | Partition reassigned; resume from last committed offset (at-least-once) |
| Kafka broker down | Replicas (RF=3) elect new leader; no loss |
| Hot partition | Salt spreads; aggregator recombines sub-keys |
| Backpressure | Kafka retains; consumers catch up; API applies backpressure not drops |

> 🎙️ Script: "Ingest scales by partitioning Kafka by video, but a viral video would hammer one partition — so for hot keys I salt the key across a few sub-partitions and recombine in the aggregator. Consumers autoscale on lag. With replication factor three, a broker death just triggers a leader election; nothing's lost."
> 🧠 If they ask "how do you know a key is hot?": "Track per-partition rate; when a key crosses a threshold, promote it to salted keying dynamically."

### How do we make the read path sub-second under thousands of QPS? (CDN/cache)

**#### Great: Materialized top-K + short-TTL cache + request coalescing**
🗣️ What this is: keep the answer precomputed and cache it for a second or two so a burst of identical reads costs one computation.
Approach: serve from a cache (1–2s TTL); on miss, a single-flight (only one request recomputes; others wait and share the result) refreshes from the shard-merge, preventing a cache-stampede. Optionally push boards to an edge/CDN for geographically distributed dashboards.
🔢 Decision-forcing math: 5K QPS with a 2s TTL = at most ~1 recompute / 2s per board instead of 5K — a 10,000x reduction on the aggregation tier.
✅ Failure matrix:

| Scenario | What happens |
|---|---|
| Cache node down | Read falls through to shard-merge; slower but correct |
| Stampede on expiry | Single-flight ensures one recompute, others share |
| Stale board during failover | `as_of` surfaces freshness; client can show "updated Xs ago" |

> 🎙️ Script: "Reads are cheap because the answer's precomputed and cached for a second or two with single-flight on misses, so a thousand simultaneous dashboard loads collapse into one recompute. That's a ten-thousand-fold cut in load on the aggregation tier, which is how we stay sub-second."
> 🧠 If they ask "why not cache longer?": "Longer TTL means a staler board; 1–2s is the sweet spot between freshness and load given our staleness budget."

## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost

**9A — Availability targets.** Read path target **three nines (99.9%)** ≈ 8.7 hours/year of allowed downtime, achieved with cached boards, multiple Redis replicas, and multi-AZ deployment (multiple data centres in one region, so if one building loses power another takes over automatically). The ingest/write path targets **four nines (99.99%)** ≈ 52 minutes/year, because losing ingest creates permanent gaps in lifetime totals — we make it more reliable than reads via Kafka replication factor 3 and producer-side buffering.

**9B — Per-dependency failure table.**

| Component | What breaks if down | Graceful degradation (what user sees) | Recovery |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ingest API | Can't accept new events | Player client retries with backoff; events buffered briefly | Autoscale/replace nodes (stateless) |
| Kafka | No durable intake | Producers buffer locally; brief backpressure | RF=3 leader election; no loss |
| Redis counters | Increments stall | Boards freeze at last value; `as_of` shows staleness | Replay from Kafka + AOF |
| Top-K shard | One slice missing | Partial board served with degraded flag | Rebuild from counters |
| Cache | More load on shards | Slightly slower reads, still correct | Cache repopulates on reads |

**9C — RPO / RTO.** RPO (Recovery Point Objective) = how much data we can lose if we crash right now. RTO (Recovery Time Objective) = how long until we're working again.

| Data class | RPO | RTO | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| All-time user totals | ≈0 (no loss) | ~minutes | Kafka RF=3 + Redis AOF; replay rebuilds |
| 15-min window | seconds (rebuildable) | ~minutes | Replay last 15m from Kafka |
| Top-K boards | 0 (derived) | seconds | Recompute from counters |
| Dedup set | tolerant of loss | n/a | Worst case a few rare double-counts |

**9D — Cost breakdown.** Top drivers: (1) **Redis RAM** — keeping ~30GB user counters + ~70GB window buckets + replicas in memory is the dominant line item, not disk; (2) **Kafka storage/throughput** — durable retention of ~1.3 TB/day; (3) **network/egress** for the dashboard reads (modest given caching). Rough order of magnitude: low-to-mid five figures/month. Biggest single optimization: **switch the 15-minute window to a Count-Min Sketch** when approximate ranking is acceptable — collapses the ~70GB window memory to a few GB.

> 🎙️ Script: "If Kafka wobbles, producers buffer and we lose nothing because of replication. If Redis dies, the boards freeze at their last value and show an 'updated X seconds ago' stamp while we replay from Kafka — the user sees stale, never wrong. The thing I protect hardest is ingest, because a lost event is a permanently wrong lifetime total, whereas a stale board self-heals in seconds."

## 10. Trade-off Ledger

**🔀 Decision: Pre-aggregate on write vs compute on read**
1. Chose pre-aggregation so reads are O(K).
2. Gave up: write-path complexity and a 1–2s lag.
3. 🗣️ Like keeping a running scoreboard updated as the game goes, instead of replaying the whole game whenever someone asks the score.
4. Reverses if: query volume were tiny and events few — then on-demand compute would be simpler.
5. 🗣️ "I do the work on the way in so the dashboard read is basically free."

**🔀 Decision: Exact counters (Redis) vs approximate sketches (Count-Min)**
1. Chose exact for lifetime totals; kept sketches as a lever for the window.
2. Gave up: memory — exact counters cost more RAM.
3. 🗣️ Like keeping every receipt's exact cents versus a very close estimate that's far cheaper to store.
4. Reverses if: the 15-min board doesn't need exactness or RAM cost spikes → swap to Count-Min Sketch.
5. 🗣️ "Lifetime totals are money-like so I keep them exact; the window I can make approximate to save a lot of memory if you're OK with tiny error."

**🔀 Decision: Sliding ring buffer vs tumbling window**
1. Chose the 900-bucket ring buffer for a true sliding window.
2. Gave up: ~900 fields per active video of memory.
3. 🗣️ Like a clock that ticks forward one second at a time instead of resetting to zero every quarter hour.
4. Reverses if: product accepts a coarse tumbling window → one counter per video, far less memory.
5. 🗣️ "I went with a ring of one-second buckets so 'last 15 minutes' actually slides; a tumbling counter would flicker to near-zero each boundary."

**🔀 Decision: At-least-once + dedup vs exactly-once delivery**
1. Chose at-least-once with an event_id dedup.
2. Gave up: a dedup store and a lookup per event.
3. 🗣️ Like stamping each receipt with a unique number so a duplicate gets ignored.
4. Reverses if: the broker offered robust exactly-once cheaply across our failure modes → drop dedup.
5. 🗣️ "Exactly-once across crashes is fragile, so I take at-least-once and make increments idempotent with an event_id."

> 🎙️ Script: "The decisions I'm least dogmatic about: I pre-aggregate on write — reverses only if traffic were tiny. I keep lifetime totals exact but I'd happily make the window approximate with a sketch to save memory if you accept small error. And I use a sliding ring buffer over a tumbling counter because the requirement literally says 'last 15 minutes,' which has to slide."

## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers

**❓ "Two events for the same video arrive at the same instant on different consumers — can the count be wrong?"**
The mechanism: increments are atomic (Redis INCRBY) so concurrent adds to the same key serialize correctly; ordering within a partition is guaranteed and cross-partition adds commute (addition is associative).
🗣️ In plain words: adding is adding — it doesn't matter what order two "+5"s happen, the total's the same, and Redis makes each add indivisible.
💬 "Counts are commutative atomic increments, so concurrency can't corrupt the total."

**❓ "What happens when Redis crashes mid-stream?"**
The mechanism: counters are backed by AOF and the Kafka log; on restart we replay uncommitted offsets, so totals are rebuilt exactly. Boards freeze at last value meanwhile.
🗣️ In plain words: nothing's truly lost because we can replay the event log; the dashboard just shows a slightly old number for a moment.
💬 "Redis is a fast cache over a durable log — we replay, we don't lose."

**❓ "A video goes viral — how do you avoid a hot partition/key?"**
The mechanism: salt the hot key across sub-partitions (video_id#0..n) so load spreads, and recombine sub-counters in the aggregate; consumers autoscale on lag.
🗣️ In plain words: we split one super-popular video's traffic across several lanes instead of jamming one lane, then add the lanes back up.
💬 "Salt hot keys to spread the firehose, recombine at aggregation."

**❓ "Can the leaderboard ever be wrong/stale?"**
The mechanism: it's eventually consistent within ~2s; the `as_of` field exposes freshness. The shard-merge is exact for K≤200.
🗣️ In plain words: the ranking's right, it might just be a second or two behind real time, and we tell you how fresh it is.
💬 "Exact ranking, bounded ~2s staleness, freshness stamped on every response."

**❓ "How do you stop someone faking a huge watch-time?"**
The mechanism: the client sends only raw duration with an authenticated token; the server validates plausibility (duration ≤ video length, rate limits per user) and never accepts a client-supplied total or rank.
🗣️ In plain words: you can't just tell us "I watched a million hours" — we only take small raw watch chunks from an authenticated player and do the math ourselves.
💬 "Server owns all aggregation; clients can't submit totals."

**❓ "What's the most expensive part and how would you cut it?"**
The mechanism: Redis RAM dominates (counters + window buckets + replicas). The biggest cut is swapping the 15-min window to a Count-Min Sketch when approximate is acceptable.
🗣️ In plain words: memory's the big bill; a compact estimator shrinks the window store from ~70GB to a few GB.
💬 "RAM's the cost driver — sketches are the lever."

**❓ "How would you add a 'top-K in the last 1 hour and last 24 hours' too?"**
The mechanism: add more bucket rings at coarser granularity (e.g., 1-minute buckets for an hour, 1-hour buckets for a day) per video — same ring-buffer pattern, different resolution; each feeds its own top-K.
🗣️ In plain words: same trick, just bigger buckets for longer windows.
💬 "Multi-resolution ring buffers — one pattern, many windows."

**❓ "What if a whole AZ goes down?"**
The mechanism: multi-AZ replicas for Redis and RF=3 Kafka across AZs mean another AZ's replicas take over; brief failover, no data loss.
🗣️ In plain words: we keep copies in multiple buildings, so one going dark just shifts traffic to another.
💬 "Multi-AZ replicas absorb a building failure with seconds of failover."

> 🎙️ 60-second verbal summary: "This is a system that ingests a firehose of video-view events and serves two real-time leaderboards — all-time top users and last-15-minutes top videos — in under a second. The design splits into two halves. The read half is dead simple: precomputed top-200 sorted sets per board, served from a one-to-two-second cache with single-flight, so thousands of dashboard reads collapse into one computation and every read is O(K). The write half is where the work lives: events land in Kafka, a durable log that absorbs three hundred thousand a second and lets us replay; consumers build exact, idempotent lifetime totals for users and a sliding 15-minute window for videos using a ring of 900 one-second buckets so old watch-time expires automatically. The hardest problem is keeping top-K exact and fast across shards, solved by per-shard local top-200s merged with a heap. The two halves connect through Kafka, which decouples the firehose from the compute. And every hard decision traces back to one principle: do the aggregation on the write path so the read is essentially free, and only trade exactness for cost where the product can tolerate it."
